cataloging_subject.label: The Chronicle attributed to Robert of Gloucester (long recension)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the Middle English verse long recension traditionally attributed to Robert of Gloucester, as represented by Thomas Hearne's hybrid text from British Library Harley MS 201 and Cotton MS Caligula A XI. It excludes the shorter recension, the physically distinct manuscripts and their adjacent works, the internally remodeled prose-and-verse text in College of Arms Arundel MS 58, Hearne's English and Latin apparatus, the 1724 and 1810 printed books as material editions, and the U-M and repository encodings as digital layers.

catalog_summary.title: The Chronicle attributed to Robert of Gloucester (long recension)
catalog_summary.author: Robert of Gloucester (attributed; identity and extent of responsibility uncertain)
catalog_summary.date: Long recension after 1297, perhaps after 1307; exact composition history uncertain
catalog_summary.region: Middle English work associated linguistically with the Worcestershire–Gloucestershire area; exact place of composition unknown

titles.value: The Chronicle attributed to Robert of Gloucester (long recension)

assertion.notes: The wording qualifies the traditional author attribution and distinguishes the longer recension represented by Hearne's source witnesses.

titles.value: The Metrical Chronicle of Robert of Gloucester

agents.name: Robert of Gloucester
notes: Traditional attributed author. Fisher finds the attribution first traceable in John Stow's 1570 edition and not securely supported by the surviving medieval evidence.
A first-person Robert reference occurs only in two witnesses and may belong to the long continuation, a continuator, or a scribe rather than a sole author of the whole chronicle.

agents.name: Thomas Hearne
notes: Editor, transcriber, and collator of the 1724 source edition; not the medieval author.

assertion.notes: The conventional attribution is retained for discovery, not promoted to established authorship.

notes: The number of contributors and the relation between the shorter and longer recensions remain uncertain.

Hearne transcribed Harley MS 201 as his main text, appended the continuation from Cotton MS Caligula A XI where Harley ends, and introduced selected readings from College of Arms Arundel MS 58 marked Ar.

scope.description: The 1724 edition's base text and continuation, not authorship of the medieval work
Selected readings and observations from Arundel MS 58 in the printed apparatus

date_statements.display: The long recension was composed or revised after 1297 and perhaps after 1307

assertion.notes: The lower bound derives from internal historical references analyzed by Fisher. The stronger post-1307 proposal remains qualified, and no single completion year is asserted.

date_statements.display: British Library Cotton MS Caligula A XI is currently cataloged to the last quarter of the thirteenth century

scope.description: The long-recension Cotton witness used for Hearne's continuation

assertion.notes: This is the current catalog date of the physical witness. Its tension with the internally argued post-1297 date of the long recension remains unresolved rather than silently harmonized.

date_statements.display: First printed by Thomas Hearne at Oxford in 1724, in two volumes

assertion.notes: This is the first publication identified in the inspected edition history; it is not a claim that no earlier excerpt ever circulated in print.

date_statements.display: Legendary foundation of Britain through events of 1271; the transmitted long text breaks off before a complete ending

places.label: Worcestershire–Gloucestershire area
notes: A cautious linguistic region inferred in scholarship; it is not an asserted writing location or a rigid modern-county boundary.

assertion.notes: Fisher summarizes the original dialect as belonging to the Worcestershire/Gloucestershire area while cautioning against overprecise localization.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Latin, Anglo-Norman, and modern English occur in Hearne's notes and editorial matter but are not modeled as intrinsic languages of the medieval verse work.

form_statements.description: A Middle English historical chronicle in rhyming couplets. The repository XML contains 12,197 direct work-text L elements in 94 LG elements, apart from one line nested inside an editorial note.

assertion.notes: The chronicle compiles and reshapes earlier historical traditions rather than serving as a neutral event record.

subject_statements.label: Kingship, succession, conquest, and political legitimacy
Henry III, baronial conflict, and the Second Barons' War

summaries.value: A Middle English verse history that opens with the geography and praise of England, begins Britain's past with Brutus, and carries a dynastic narrative through British, Roman, Anglo-Saxon, Danish, Norman, Angevin, and thirteenth-century rule. Its later sections give extended attention to the Norman Conquest, Stephen and Matilda, Henry III, Simon de Montfort, and baronial conflict. The transmitted long text ends incompletely during the aftermath of the murder of Henry of Almain in 1271.

Hearne's represented text has two source-defined segments: pp. 1–464 from Harley MS 201, ending during Stephen's reign as the Empress Matilda escapes Oxford, and pp. 465–571 from Cotton MS Caligula A XI, continuing into Henry III's reign and breaking off in the events of 1271. The boundary is explicit in the edition and XML but is not treated as an authorial book division.

The XML's title page names Hearne's 1724 publication, but page images and the colophon at printed p. 364 show that its immediate typographic source is Samuel Bagster's 1810 reset reprint. The medieval work, Hearne's 1724 edition, Bagster's 1810 reprint, and the digital encodings are therefore kept as distinct descriptive layers.

extent.description: The repository encoding has 94 LG elements and 12,198 L elements in total. Excluding one L nested inside an editorial NOTE leaves 12,197 direct work-text lines: 9,679 in the Harley-based main segment and 2,518 in the Cotton-based continuation. It has 570 PB elements overall (569 in BODY), two BODY DIV1 elements, and one supplement DIV2. These are encoding-specific counts, not a claim about canonical medieval lineation or authorial sectioning.

content_parts.label: Harley-based main text: Brutus through the Empress Matilda's escape from Oxford
content_parts.summary: The proem, geography and praise of England, legendary British history, Roman and Anglo-Saxon rule, the Danish and Norman periods, and the chronicle through the point in Stephen's reign where Harley MS 201 ends.
notes: The part boundary describes Hearne's witness splice, not a recovered medieval division.

content_parts.label: Cotton-based continuation: Stephen through events of 1271
content_parts.summary: The continuation supplied where Harley ends, extending the history through Henry II and his successors, Magna Carta and its aftermath, Henry III, Simon de Montfort and baronial conflict, and the murder of Henry of Almain before the surviving text breaks off.

locators.value: chronicle at ff. 3r–13v and 15r–168v; Hearne's printed head cites f. 130b at the splice

notes: Cotton Caligula A XI is imperfect and also contains unrelated works; only its Robert of Gloucester component belongs to this source role.

related_works.title: The Chronicle attributed to Robert of Gloucester (shorter recension)
notes: Scholarship distinguishes two recensions whose relationship and stages of composition remain debated. The shorter recension is outside CME00022's cataloged unit.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00022.xml.
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for identifiers, Middle English language, reproduced 1724 title page, Hearne's two source segments, complete encoded text on printed pp. 1–571, division labels, embedded apparatus, closing, structure, checksum, Git blob, and 2016 review history.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CME00022, Robert of Gloucester's Chronicle.
evidence.summary: Official collection evidence for the stable CME identity, title and current corpus context. The item handle returned an automated-access denial during the 2026-07-12 check, so item-level production claims are not inferred from generic collection statements.

evidence.citation: Thomas Hearne, ed., Robert of Gloucester's Chronicle, in The Works of Thomas Hearne, vols. 1–2 (London: printed for Samuel Bagster, 1810), complete Wellcome Library scans.
evidence.locator: vol. 1 scan pp. 13, 64–78, 99–462; vol. 2 scan pp. 15–219; printed Chronicle pp. 1–571
evidence.summary: Immediate-source evidence for the retained 1724 title page, Bagster's 1810 series title, Hearne's witness statements and editorial method, complete encoded Chronicle pages, source splice at p. 465, and the Mercier and Chervet reset-print colophon at p. 364.

evidence.citation: Thomas Hearne, ed., Robert of Gloucester's Chronicle, 2 vols. (Oxford: printed at the Theater, 1724), complete ECCO scans.
evidence.locator: vol. 1 title and preface, especially pp. lii–lxvi, and Chronicle pp. 1–364; vol. 2 Chronicle pp. 367–571
evidence.summary: First-edition evidence for Hearne's title, imprint, source selection, transcription from Harley, continuation from Cotton, selective Arundel collation, and original page sequence. Direct comparison shows that the XML follows the 1810 reset rather than these 1724 types and page layouts.

evidence.citation: British Library, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 201, Robert of Gloucester, Chronicle of English history, record 040-002046029.
evidence.summary: Repository evidence for shelfmark, title, ff. 4r–161r work extent, first-quarter-fifteenth-century date, Middle English rhyming couplets, English production, physical description, misbinding, use by Hearne, and its relationship to the Cotton text.

evidence.citation: British Library, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Caligula A XI, ff. 3–13, 15–169, record 041-001102341.
evidence.summary: Repository evidence for the component shelfmark, Robert of Gloucester Chronicle at ff. 3r–13v and 15r–168v, first-version and imperfect status, current last-quarter-thirteenth-century date, physical support and extent, 1731 fire damage, and adjacent unrelated codex contents.

evidence.citation: AIM25, College of Arms, Arundel Manuscripts, description of Arundel MS 58.
evidence.summary: Repository-level description of Arundel MS 58 as a fifteenth-century History of England that remodels Robert of Gloucester's metrical chronicle with prose and interpolations from other historical sources and continues to 1332.

evidence.citation: Matthew Fisher, Once Called Albion: The Composition and Transmission of History Writing in England, 1280–1350 (DPhil thesis, University of Oxford, 2005), chapters 3–4.
evidence.summary: Specialist evidence for the uncertain Robert attribution, fifteen-witness and two-recension model, post-1297 and possible post-1307 long-recension date, Worcestershire–Gloucestershire dialect association, incomplete transmitted ending, and Arundel MS 58's substantial remodeling.

evidence.citation: XPath and structural inspection of CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00022.xml.
evidence.locator: count(//DIV1), count(//DIV2), count(//HEAD), count(//LG), count(//L), count(//NOTE), count(//P), count(//PB), direct work-line counts, and TEXT/@LANG
evidence.summary: Reproducibly finds 2 DIV1, 1 supplement DIV2, 1 HEAD, 94 LG, 12,198 L, 3,834 NOTE, 18 P, and 570 PB elements, with TEXT/@LANG='enm'. Excluding one L nested in NOTE yields 12,197 direct work lines: 9,679 before the supplement and 2,518 within it. These are encoding metrics rather than canonical lineation.

open_questions.question: Who composed and revised the Chronicle's two recensions, and how should the internally argued post-1297 date of the long recension be reconciled with the British Library's current 1275–1300 dating of Cotton Caligula A XI?
next_steps: Consult post-2005 scholarship on recension chronology, the Robert self-reference, and Cotton paleography.
Ask the British Library whether the component date reflects a recent codicological reassessment and whether a narrower date is supportable.
Keep the traditional Robert attribution qualified and the two date layers distinct until the conflict is resolved.

open_questions.question: Can complete, reusable digital facsimiles be obtained for Harley MS 201, Cotton MS Caligula A XI, and College of Arms Arundel MS 58?
next_steps: Ask the British Library about unindexed photography or digitization for both manuscripts and request complete physical-object coverage if images must be ordered.
Ask the College of Arms about existing full photography of Arundel MS 58, costs, retention, and reuse terms.
Map work portions only after complete-object coverage and exact folio/canvas sequences are verified.
notes: The lineage manifest records the dated search scope and mediated physical-access routes; no manuscript image was cached.

open_questions.question: What physical copy, initial keying date and method, correction history, and source-file terms apply to the U-M CME00022 encoding?
next_steps: Ask the U-M Digital Collections team for item-level production metadata, copy provenance, and any downloadable source package.
Do not transfer generic collection-level manual-keying statements to this item without item evidence.
Keep 2016-09-23 scoped to the documented TEMPHEAD review rather than encoding creation.

open_questions.question: How much text is missing from the end of the long recension and from Cotton Caligula A XI, and did any medieval copy ever preserve the intended ending through 1272?
next_steps: Compare all long-recension witnesses and modern critical editions at the ending.
Separate Hearne's belief about an intended 1272 endpoint from recoverable witness evidence.
Inspect Cotton directly or in a complete facsimile for the missing-leaf sequence and terminal codicology.

notes: The preferred title retains the traditional association with Robert of Gloucester but explicitly qualifies authorship and fixes the cataloging boundary at the long recension.
The work's uncertain composition history, witness dates, Hearne's 1724 edition, Bagster's 1810 reset, and the U-M encoding are not collapsed into one date or artifact.
Harley MS 201 supplies Hearne's main text, Cotton MS Caligula A XI supplies the continuation, and Arundel MS 58 supplies selected apparatus readings; these are distinct source roles.
The 12,197-line and two-part description is an encoding-based navigational summary, not an assertion of canonical medieval lineation or authorial books.
No complete public facsimile of any of the three manuscripts was verified on 2026-07-12.
